data.title: Vimeo announces global layoffs after Bending Spoons acquisition
data.description:
• Vimeo (VMEO) has initiated global layoffs shortly after its acquisition by Italian tech firm Bending Spoons, marking the platform's second round of staff cuts in under six months.
  • The cutbacks will impact a “large portion” of the company’s total workforce. The former VP of Global Brand and Creative, Dave Brown, said that he, “along with a large portion of the company,” had been “impacted by layoffs.”
  • Bending Spoons completed the $1.38B all-cash acquisition of Vimeo on November 24, 2025, taking the company private at $7.85 per share.

data.title: T-Mobile US to redeem $3 billion of 4.750% senior notes due 2028
data.description:
T-Mobile US Inc. (TMUS) said on Thursday that its wholly owned subsidiary, T-Mobile USA, will redeem on Feb. 1, 2026, the full $1.5 billion outstanding aggregate principal amount of its 4.750% Senior Notes due 2028, as well as the full $1.5 billion outstanding aggregate principal amount of its 4.750% Senior Notes due 2028-1 held by Deutsche Telekom AG.

The notes will be redeemed at a price equal to 100% of the principal amount, plus accrued and unpaid interest up to, but not including, the redemption date.

Payment of the redemption price for the 2028 notes will be made through the facilities of The Depository Trust Company. Deutsche Bank Trust Company Americas is the trustee and paying agent for the notes.

data.title: Petco prices $600M senior secured notes to strengthen capital structure
data.description:
• Petco (WOOF) Thursday reported the pricing of its previously announced senior secured notes offering and has agreed to issue and sell $600,000,000 in aggregate principal amount of new senior secured notes due on February 1, 2031, which will bear interest at 8.250% each year.
  • Completion of the notes offering is expected to occur on February 2, 2026, and is subject to, among other things, customary closing conditions.
  • Petco (WOOF) intends to use the net proceeds from the sale of the notes, together with borrowings under a new term loan facility and cash on hand, to repay in full its existing term loan facility, to pay related fees and expenses, and for general corporate purposes.

data.title: Japan’s annual inflation tumbles to 2.1% in December; core inflation hits 14-month low
data.description:
Japan’s annual inflation rate decreased to 2.1% in December 2025, down from 2.9% in November and the lowest since March 2022. Meanwhile, food inflation dropped to 5.1%, while core inflation fell to 2.4%, still above the central bank's target.

Food prices in Japan increased by 5.1% year-on-year in December 2025, slowing from a 6.1% gain in the previous month and marking the slowest rise since November 2024. 

Core inflation slipped to 2.4% from 3.0%, the lowest since October 2024, though still above the central bank’s 2% target for the 45th straight month. Monthly, CPI fell 0.1%, reversing November’s 0.3% gain and marking the first drop in nine months.

Separately, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is preparing to dissolve parliament and call a snap election to consolidate power and pursue increased spending.

The Nikkei 225 Index rose 0.2% to around 53,790, while the broader Topix Index also gained 0.2% to 3,624 on Friday, extending gains from the previous session. The Japanese yen depreciated to around 158.7 per dollar on Friday, extending this week’s decline after the Bank of Japan kept its policy rate unchanged as widely expected, after raising it to a 30-year high of 0.75% last month.

More on Japan's economy:

data.title: Slm outlines 12%-14% origination growth for 2026 while launching new $500M share repurchase program
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SLM Corporation (SLM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jonathan Witter reported, "I'm pleased to report on a successful year and discuss our strong outlook for 2026. Overall, the private student lending sector remains robust and is positioned for further success." He emphasized the impact of federal student lending reforms, stating these changes could contribute an estimated $5 billion in annual originations for Sallie Mae, representing approximately 70% originations growth over 2025 when fully phased in.
  • Witter highlighted a new private credit strategic partnership, describing it as "an innovative first-of-its-kind agreement...combines the more predictable earnings profile of our bank with the capital efficiency and risk transfer benefits of our loan sale program."
  • GAAP diluted EPS for the fourth quarter was $1.12, and for the full year $3.46, compared to $2.68 in 2024. Private education loan originations for the full year were $7.4 billion, a 6% increase over 2024.
  • CFO Peter Graham announced, "We continued our capital return strategy in the fourth quarter repurchasing 3.8 million shares for $106 million for a total of 12.8 million shares for $373 million over the full year 2025." He further stated, "Tonight, we are announcing a new 2-year $500 million authorization."
  • Graham noted, "Our net interest margin was 5.21% for the quarter, 29 basis points higher than the prior year period and 5.24% for the full year, up 5 basis points year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Witter stated, "We expect full year 2026 private education loan origination growth of 12% to 14%." Expected noninterest expenses for the full year 2026 are projected to be between $750 million and $780 million, with increased costs attributed to growth, one-time investments, and higher marketing and acquisition costs.
  • After 2026, the company expects to grow the bank portfolio gradually by roughly 1 to 2 percentage points per year until reaching a steady state annual growth rate in the mid-single digits. Approximately 30% to 40% of private student lending originations are estimated to flow through strategic partnerships.
  • Full year 2026 diluted earnings per common share is expected to be between $2.70 and $2.80.
  • SLM anticipates net charge-offs for the total loan portfolio to be between $345 million and $385 million for 2026.

Financial Results

  • GAAP diluted EPS in Q4 was $1.12 and $3.46 for the full year. Private education loan originations were $1.02 billion in Q4 and $7.4 billion for the year.
  • Net charge-offs for the private education loan portfolio were $98 million for Q4 and $346 million for the full year, representing 2.15% of average private education loans in repayment, down 4 basis points from 2024.
  • Net interest margin for Q4 was 5.21% and 5.24% for the year.
  • Delinquent private education loans (30 days or more) represented 4% of loans in repayment at year-end, unchanged from Q3.
  • Noninterest expenses for the full year were $659 million, a 2.6% increase year-over-year.
  • Liquidity at quarter-end was 18.6% of total assets, with total risk-based capital at 12.4% and common equity Tier 1 capital at 11.1%.

Q&A

  • Caroline Latta, BofA Securities: Asked about the impact of the postponement of wage garnishment and treasury offset on loan performance. Witter responded that the impact would be minimal due to differences in customer bases.
  • Latta followed up on modeling growth from the grad PLUS opportunity. Graham explained the incremental volume in 2026 will be modest and included in current guidance, with more significant increases expected over the next 2 to 3 years.
  • Giuliano Anderes-Bologna, Compass Point: Inquired about partnership and loan sale volumes. Graham cited a minimum $2 billion commitment for the strategic partnership and estimated about 30% of originations will be designated for sale, with seasonality affecting quarterly figures.
  • Terry Ma, Barclays: Questioned the return on increased noninterest expenses. Witter described the current investment as a "once-in-a-lifetime opportunity" and emphasized strong governance and measurement around ROI.
  • Ma also asked about credit assumptions. Graham noted that 75% of 2023 modification cohort borrowers are current at year-end, with this performance reflected in 2026 charge-off guidance.
  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC: Asked about EPS growth visibility into 2027. Witter said visibility hinges on the TAM opportunity and expense management discipline.
  • Melissa Wedel, JPMorgan: Raised questions about gain on sale margins and the share repurchase program. Graham commented on seasonal and transactional variability in gain on sale margins and outlined the disciplined approach to buybacks under the new authorization.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions around loan performance, expense growth, partnership volumes, and credit assumptions, with a neutral to slightly cautious tone, seeking clarity on the impacts of strategic changes and forward-looking investments.
  • Management remained confident and constructive, emphasizing strategic opportunities, expense discipline, and capital return, with Witter stating, "we are excited about our company's future and the opportunities ahead."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained focused on credit trends and strategic partnership implementation, while management displayed increased optimism about growth opportunities and strategic positioning.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from reaffirming 2025 outlook to projecting 12%-14% origination growth in 2026, with higher noninterest expenses expected due to investments and marketing.
  • Strategic focus evolved from announcing a partnership to operationalizing it, with about 30%-40% of originations set to flow through these partnerships.
  • Management tone grew more optimistic about the PLUS reform opportunity and long-term growth, while analysts continued to probe on credit quality and cost management.
  • Key metrics such as net charge-offs, delinquency, and originations were stable or improved, with detailed context provided for changes due to new partnership strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted an increase in early-stage delinquencies but downplayed its predictive value for future charge-offs, citing stable late-stage delinquencies and improved collections.
  • The transition to selling newly originated loans and holding loans for sale each quarter is expected to shift reported metrics, but Graham emphasized this was "primarily driven by calculation mechanics rather than a change in the underlying performance."
  • Analysts highlighted risks regarding elevated operating expenses, credit performance in a challenging job market, and the successful scaling of new partnership models.

Final Takeaway

SLM Corporation concluded the year highlighting strong execution, strategic innovation with its private credit partnership, and confidence in capturing the PLUS reform opportunity. The company projects significant origination growth of 12%-14% in 2026 and is investing ahead of anticipated volume, while maintaining a disciplined approach to capital returns with a new $500 million share repurchase authorization. Management underscored the stability of credit performance and efficiency, while preparing for a structural shift toward partnerships and a more capital-efficient business model, positioning SLM for continued value creation in the coming years.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Capital One outlines $5.15B Brex acquisition and signals near-term upward pressure on efficiency ratio amid strategic investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Capital One Financial Corporation (COF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Richard Fairbank, Founder, Chairman, CEO & President, announced the definitive agreement to acquire Brex for a combination of stock and cash totaling $5.15 billion. Fairbank explained, "Brex is a pioneer in the dynamically changing business payment space, with industry-leading technology and world-class talent. Acquiring Brex accelerates a journey we've been on since our founding days, the quest to build a banking and payments company that's positioned to win where the world is going."
  • Fairbank highlighted the ongoing integration of Discover and stated that 2025 was "a seminal year for Capital One," emphasizing the completed acquisition of Discover and the strategic opportunities it unlocks, particularly in growing and scaling the global payments network.
  • Fairbank noted, "We remain on track to deliver the expected synergies" from the Discover integration and signaled that the Brex acquisition "will have no impact on the Discover integration or expected synergies."
  • Andrew Young, Chief Financial Officer, reported, "In the fourth quarter, Capital One earned $2.1 billion or $3.26 per diluted common share. For the full year, Capital One earned $2.5 billion or $4.03 per share."
  • Young also highlighted completion of the $8.8 billion Discover home loans portfolio sale, resulting in a net gain on sale of $483 million.

Outlook

  • Management indicated continued investment in growth opportunities, especially in technology, AI, and the premium credit card space, which is expected to result in "some upward pressure on efficiency ratio in the near term."
  • Fairbank stated, "We still expect our earnings power on the other side of the Discover integration to be consistent with what we expected at the time we announced the Discover deal, inclusive of Brex."
  • The company plans to accelerate Brex's growth "almost from day 1" post-close and is leaning into strategic investments that include Capital One Travel, Capital One Shopping, and Auto Navigator.

Financial Results

  • Reported fourth quarter revenue increased about 1% compared to the prior quarter, with noninterest expense up 13%.
  • Adjusted fourth quarter earnings per share were $3.86, and full year adjusted earnings per share were $19.61.
  • The provision for credit losses was $4.1 billion, an increase of about $1.4 billion relative to the third quarter, driven by a $302 million allowance build and a $360 million increase in net charge-offs.
  • The allowance balance ended at $23.4 billion, and the coverage ratio stood at 5.16%.
  • Net interest margin for the quarter was 8.26%, a decline of 10 basis points from the prior quarter.
  • The common equity Tier 1 capital ratio was 14.3%, about 10 basis points lower than the prior quarter after $2.5 billion in share repurchases.

Q&A

  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: Asked about the strategic value of Brex and the 10% credit card cap proposal. Fairbank detailed Brex’s integrated platform and cautioned that a credit card rate cap "would make credit much less available for consumers up and down the credit spectrum."
  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Queried about the Credit Card Competition Act and global acceptance. Fairbank emphasized the importance of building international acceptance for the Discover network, stating, "It's a long journey to build acceptance all over the world."
  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about investment acceleration and efficiency management. Fairbank responded, "The net effect will be some upward pressure on efficiency ratio in the near term... we still expect our earnings power... to be consistent with what we expected at the time we announced the Discover deal, inclusive of Brex."
  • Terry Ma, Barclays: Asked about consumer health and loan growth brownout. Fairbank noted, "The brownout... will continue until the card integration is done," but affirmed that "the U.S. consumer and the overall macro economy remain resilient."
  • John Pancari, Evercore: Pressed for Brex deal financials and timing. Young explained, "Given the relative size of Brex to Capital One, we don't intend to provide additional metrics," while Fairbank asserted that Brex and Discover integrations can proceed in parallel.
  • Saul Martinez, HSBC: Sought clarity on Brex’s impact on earnings and buybacks. Fairbank confirmed, "Brex will result in earnings dilution initially as we're buying a business with a growth rate that is multiples of industry growth rates," and Young stated, "The Brex transaction itself will take down our capital by, I think, a little more than 40 basis points... but certainly not enough to influence our thinking about near-term repurchases."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was probing and cautious, especially around the Brex acquisition, efficiency outlook, and regulatory risks, with repeated questions on financial impacts, capital returns, and strategic integration.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone, frequently emphasizing strategic alignment and long-term value, though occasionally acknowledged near-term dilution and expense pressure. Fairbank repeatedly used phrases such as "we are leaning into them" and "we still expect our earnings power" to reinforce confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst questions remained focused on growth investments and integration, but showed increased skepticism around incremental acquisitions and capital deployment. Management’s tone was similarly confident, but with more direct acknowledgment of near-term headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The fourth quarter introduced the Brex acquisition, a major development not present in the previous quarter, alongside continued focus on Discover integration.
  • Adjusted earnings per share declined from $5.95 in Q3 to $3.86 in Q4.
  • Provision for credit losses rose by $1.4 billion quarter-over-quarter, reversing the prior quarter’s allowance release.
  • Marketing and noninterest expenses accelerated, reflecting heightened investment activity compared to Q3.
  • Management reiterated its long-term earnings outlook, while analysts intensified their scrutiny of capital allocation and the impact of new deals.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited competitive intensity, temporary loan growth headwinds in the Discover portfolio, and ongoing regulatory risks such as proposed credit card rate caps and the Credit Card Competition Act.
  • Analysts raised concerns about earnings dilution from the Brex acquisition and near-term efficiency ratio pressure.
  • Fairbank addressed these by emphasizing the temporary nature of certain headwinds and the company’s disciplined investment approach.

Final Takeaway

Capital One signaled its commitment to strategic growth through the Brex acquisition, ongoing Discover integration, and investments in technology and premium customer segments. Management acknowledged near-term expense and efficiency pressure, but emphasized the enduring earnings power and long-term value creation expected from these initiatives, underscoring a disciplined approach to capital deployment and an optimistic outlook for future synergies and market expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alcoa expects $10/ton CBAM premium uplift in Europe for 2026 while advancing San Ciprián restart
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alcoa Corporation (AA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • William Oplinger, President and CEO, reported "strong operational performance and stability, achieving annual production records at 5 of our smelters and 1 refinery" and highlighted robust financial performance and cash generation. Oplinger emphasized progress on strategic initiatives, including the restart of the San Ciprián smelter, which reached approximately 65% operational capacity by year-end 2025, and expected completion in the first half of 2026. Negotiations are ongoing regarding the monetization of transformation sites in the U.S., with an agreement targeted for the first half of 2026. Oplinger also noted the successful start-up of the 450 kA inert anode cell at ELYSIS, calling it "a defining moment in the transition towards large-scale, low-carbon aluminum production."
  • Oplinger stated, "Alcoa closed the fourth quarter with strong operational and financial performance, supported by improved safety results and record production across multiple assets. Our momentum continues with progress on the company's strategic initiatives aimed at creating further value in 2026."
  • Molly Beerman, Executive VP & CFO, reported, "Revenue increased 15% sequentially to $3.4 billion." Beerman stated that net income attributable to Alcoa was $226 million with earnings per share of $0.85. She highlighted a noncash charge of $144 million to impair goodwill in the Alumina segment, and a tax benefit of $133 million from the reversal of a valuation allowance on deferred tax assets in Brazil. Adjusted net income was $335 million or $1.26 per share, with adjusted EBITDA of $546 million.
  • Beerman stated, "We ended December with a strong cash balance of $1.6 billion." She noted free cash flow for the year at $594 million and highlighted the reduction in working capital contributing to fourth quarter free cash flow. Beerman confirmed, "We finished the year at $1.5 billion of adjusted net debt, reaching the high end of our target range of $1 billion to $1.5 billion."

Outlook

  • Beerman provided 2026 full-year outlook: alumina production between 9.7 million and 9.9 million tons, shipments between 11.8 million and 12.0 million tons. Aluminum production is expected between 2.4 million and 2.6 million tons; shipments between 2.6 million and 2.8 million tons, reflecting increases from the San Ciprián restart. Transformation costs are expected to be $100 million. Other corporate expense will increase to approximately $160 million. Depreciation is forecast at $630 million, nonoperating pension and OPEB expense at $35 million, and interest expense at $140 million. Capital expenditures are planned at $750 million, with environmental and ARO spending expected to rise to $325 million.
  • For Q1 2026, Beerman expects alumina performance to be unfavorable by approximately $30 million and aluminum segment performance to be unfavorable by $70 million, citing the non-recurrence of Spain and Norway CO2 compensation credits and additional San Ciprián restart costs.
  • Oplinger said, "We expect CBAM to generate a net positive impact of approximately $10 per metric ton in 2026, with the uplift in the Rotterdam premium outweighing our carbon cost increases."

Financial Results

  • Beerman reported a sequential revenue increase to $3.4 billion and an adjusted EBITDA of $546 million. She cited higher metal prices and increased shipments as key drivers. The Alumina segment saw a decrease in adjusted EBITDA by $36 million, while the Aluminum segment's adjusted EBITDA increased by $213 million. Cash balance at year-end was $1.6 billion, and free cash flow for the fourth quarter was $294 million. The company returned $105 million to stockholders through a quarterly dividend.
  • Return on equity for the year was 16.4%. Beerman indicated a reduction in working capital by 15 days sequentially.

Q&A

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley, asked about confidence in 2026 guidance and potential for upward revisions. Oplinger responded, “We think the 2026 guidance is very much attainable. It's going to be based on how some of the restarts around the system go. We're in the process of restarting San Ciprián, we're still working on the restart of São Luís and overall... we're confident that we can continue that progress.”
  • Giles inquired about domestic alumina supply and the gallium project. Oplinger replied that Alcoa would consider U.S.-based alumina supply if it reduces transportation costs and reported ongoing progress with the gallium project.
  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley, focused on alumina segment profitability and cost reduction. Oplinger stated, "We have a low-cost position on the cost curve, and there are other plants around the world, specifically in China, who are much higher on the cost curve."
  • De Alba asked for updates on idle site monetization. Beerman explained, “The negotiations for the primary site that we're working on now... could involve a multiyear payment stream as well as some value sharing structures. We are continuing to progress several other sites. We have 10 priority sites in total to meet our target of $500 million to $1 billion over the next 5 years.”
  • Katja Jancic, BMO, inquired about Alumar and San Ciprián. Oplinger acknowledged setbacks at Alumar due to power interruptions but expects stable production in Q1 2026. Beerman noted that San Ciprián smelter is expected to reach profitability after the restart, with an EBITDA loss of $75 million to $100 million for 2026, mainly at the refinery, and outlined a plan to reach cash neutrality in 2027.
  • Glyn Lawcock, Barrenjoey, sought updates on mine moves and Canadian tariff exemption. Oplinger confirmed mine approvals progress and stated, "It's difficult to say whether there will be a Canadian exemption. Midwest premium obviously has risen to cover the total tariff expense."
  • Lawson Winder, BofA, asked about alumina productivity improvements and capital returns. Beerman attributed productivity gains to technical efforts amid lower bauxite grades. She noted, “We do expect to generate cash across 2026 and that will be used for additional debt repayments.”
  • Timna Tanners, Wells Fargo, discussed U.S. and European production volumes. Oplinger noted the high cost and long lead time for restarting the idle Warrick line and said, “In Europe, it all comes down to... energy. And what are energy prices doing? And last time I looked, energy is not getting significantly cheaper in Europe anytime soon.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly constructive, with questions focusing on guidance achievability, profitability in challenged segments, productivity improvements, and capital allocation. Several questions probed for detail on execution risk and timing of strategic initiatives.
  • Management maintained a confident and composed tone, especially in prepared remarks, but was slightly more cautious during Q&A regarding challenges at Alumar and San Ciprián, mine approval processes, and the complexity of site monetization. Phrases like “we believe...” and “we are confident...” indicated steady but measured optimism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst questions shifted slightly more toward execution risk on restarts and profitability, while management showed increased confidence in operational momentum but greater caution regarding external market and regulatory uncertainties.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The fourth quarter featured a sharp rebound in revenue and EBITDA driven by higher aluminum prices and increased shipments, in contrast to the previous quarter’s modest decline. Guidance for 2026 is more detailed, with specific production and shipment targets for both alumina and aluminum, and explicit commentary on transformation and environmental costs.
  • Management’s tone in Q4 was more assertive regarding progress on restarts, site monetization, and strategic initiatives, while continuing to highlight operational risks and external factors. Analysts shifted focus from capital allocation and M&A in Q3 to execution on restarts, profitability in challenged assets, and market-driven risks in Q4.
  • The outlook for San Ciprián is more transparent, with explicit EBITDA loss and cash flow expectations. Market commentary on CBAM and tariffs is more granular, with new quantification of expected financial impact.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing pressure on alumina prices and the profitability of that segment, with exposure to low spot pricing and cost curve positioning.
  • Power interruptions and operational instability at Alumar remain a challenge, though stabilization efforts are underway.
  • Regulatory and permitting risks persist for mine moves in Western Australia, with approvals expected by year-end 2026.
  • Site monetization negotiations are complex and may extend over multiple years, potentially affecting the timing of proceeds.
  • Tariff and geopolitical risks continue to influence U.S.-Canada trade flows, with the company passing costs through to customers but lacking clarity on future exemptions.

Final Takeaway

Alcoa closed 2025 with record production, strong cash generation, and a strengthened balance sheet, supported by favorable aluminum market dynamics and operational improvements. Strategic priorities for 2026 include the completion of the San Ciprián restart, disciplined capital allocation, and advancing site monetization. The company projects a net positive financial impact from CBAM in Europe, while remaining focused on operational stability and navigating market, regulatory, and cost challenges in its core segments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Columbia Banking System outlines NIM surpassing 4% in 2026 with increased buybacks and Pac Premier integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Columbia Banking System, Inc. (COLB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Clint Stein described the fourth quarter as a strong conclusion to the year, highlighting the completion of the Pacific Premier Bank acquisition: "Pac Premier was the missing puzzle piece to complete our Western footprint. The acquisition bolstered our position as the preeminent regional bank in the Northwest and improved our competitive position in other key Western markets, most notably Southern California, where we now hold a top 10 deposit market share position."
  • Stein emphasized continued expansion, noting new locations in Arizona, Colorado, California, and Oregon, and signaled ongoing targeted de novo activity in 2026, funded by savings from a 2024 expense initiative.
  • The CEO cited a 27% quarter-over-quarter increase in operating pre-provision net revenue (PPNR), attributing this to the Pac Premier integration and balance sheet optimization, and stated: "Our bankers have worked tirelessly to generate consistent, repeatable earnings for 8 consecutive quarters. Consistency has long been a historical trend for Columbia, and we expect that trend to continue as we go forward."
  • CFO Ivan Seda reported: "On an operating basis, which excludes merger expense and other items detailed in our non-GAAP disclosure, fourth quarter pre-provision net revenue and operating net income increased 27% and 19%, respectively, compared to the prior quarter, while full year 2025 results rose 22% and 31% compared to 2024."
  • Seda highlighted a net interest margin of 4.06% for Q4, up from 3.84% in Q3, and described the benefit from $12 million in premium amortization related to acquired time deposits and $5 million from accelerated loan repayment, noting these will not recur in 2026.
  • Seda also noted the achievement of $63 million in annualized deal-related cost savings, about 50% of the targeted $127 million, with additional cost savings expected in the first half of 2026.

Outlook

  • Seda projected a net interest margin for Q1 2026 in the range of 3.90% to 3.95%, stating: "After bottoming out in the first quarter, we expect net interest margin to trend higher each quarter throughout 2026 as customer deposit balances rebound and balance sheet optimization actions continue to improve profitability, ultimately surpassing 4% net interest margin in the second or third quarter of the year."
  • Columbia expects noninterest expense (excluding CDI amortization) to remain in the $335 million to $345 million range in Q1 and Q2, declining modestly in Q3 as all Pac Premier cost savings are realized by end of Q2.
  • Share repurchase activity is expected to increase to a range of $150 million to $200 million per quarter in 2026, with $600 million authorized remaining on the current plan.

Financial Results

  • Columbia reported Q4 EPS of $0.72 and operating EPS of $0.82, increases of 6% and 15%, respectively, from the prior year's Q4.
  • Noninterest income was $90 million on a GAAP basis and $88 million on an operating basis, with $13 million of the sequential increase attributed to Pac Premier.
  • Operating noninterest expense was $373 million, up $66 million from the prior quarter, of which $62 million was Pac Premier-related. Excluding CDI amortization, expense was $331 million, at the lower end of the expected range.
  • Provision expense was $23 million for the quarter. Allowance for credit losses ended at 1.02% of loans, or 1.32% including credit discount on acquired loans.
  • Gross loans and leases were $47.8 billion, down from $48.5 billion in Q3, and total deposits were $54.2 billion compared to $55.8 billion in Q3.
  • Dividend increased to $0.37 per share and 3.7 million shares were repurchased at an average price of $27.07. CET1 and total risk-based capital ratios rose to 11.8% and 13.6%, respectively. Tangible book value increased to $19.11.

Q&A

  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC: Asked about Pac Premier integration and growth. Stein responded that excitement and cultural integration have been strong, with significant new business opportunities in larger commercial segments and product cross-selling. Torran Nixon added, "the enthusiasm and excitement from the Pac Premier folks has just been amazing," citing several large new client wins.
  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Inquired about loan runoff and retention. Seda explained that roughly 70%-80% of transactional loans are being retained as they reprice, and Nixon detailed that 85% of construction loans exiting are being refinanced by Fannie or Freddie.
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Sought full-year loan growth outlook. Seda stated, "Pretty flat is our current outlook."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked about loan sale gains and future appetite. Frank Namdar said the $45 million in loan sales were Pac Premier adversely rated loans, sold at carrying value, and Seda added that future sales would be "more surgical opportunities."
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: Questioned NII trajectory. Seda indicated NII would dip below $600 million in Q1 before increasing in Q2 and beyond.
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked about earning assets and NIM. Seda confirmed earning assets are expected in the $60.5 billion to $61 billion range, with NIM surpassing 4% in Q2 or Q3 and continuing upward.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts posed questions focused on integration, cost savings, deposit trends, and expense guidance, displaying a neutral to slightly positive tone and seeking clarifications on execution and sustainability of recent gains.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases like "we are very pleased" and "we expect that trend to continue." Stein and Seda repeatedly emphasized consistency, operational success, and future opportunities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence appeared higher, particularly around the Pac Premier integration and the company's earnings trajectory.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language sharpened: Q4 included a specific net interest margin trajectory and explicit cost-saving targets, while Q3 guidance was more cautious with less numerical specificity.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward realizing Pac Premier synergies and increasing capital returns through higher buybacks versus Q3’s emphasis on the newly closed acquisition and initial integration.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on loan growth dynamics, deposit trends, and expense management, but Q4 questions reflected more attention to execution and sustainability of cost saves and capital returns.
  • Key metric changes included higher net interest margin, increased noninterest income, and higher tangible book value in Q4. Management’s tone moved from cautiously optimistic in Q3 to more assured and forward-looking in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited intentional reduction of certain deposits and seasonal outflows as contributors to deposit decline, with expectations of further contraction in Q1.
  • Seda warned that the 11-basis-point benefit to NIM from time deposit premium amortization will not repeat in 2026.
  • Pac Premier loan sales were described as a "cleanup execution" unlikely to recur at scale, but management will continue evaluating for smaller, targeted opportunities.
  • Risk of modest earning asset contraction in Q1 was flagged, with mitigation through core lending growth and optimization.
  • Substandard loans increased slightly, offset by a decrease in special mention loans, but Namdar noted this was due to elongated downturns rather than degrading performance.

Final Takeaway

Columbia Banking System’s fourth quarter underscored the operational and financial benefits from the Pac Premier acquisition, highlighted by a rising net interest margin, robust cost savings, and an expanding capital return program. Management expects further NIM improvement in 2026, with a focus on disciplined growth, continued integration, and enhanced shareholder returns through increased buybacks, while maintaining strong credit metrics and pursuing balance sheet optimization.
